Yes, it is possible to use a different `create_app` function when running your Flask app as a server versus when running a CLI command. However, the standard Flask CLI command system does not natively support passing different `create_app` configurations based on the command itself. But you can achieve this by using a combination of environment variables and custom logic to determine the context in which the CLI command is being run.

You can define a CLI command using the `@app.cli.command` decorator, and within the command function, you can check for the presence of a specific environment variable. This variable can be used to determine whether the CLI command is being run in a different context than the server. For instance, you could use `FLASK_CLI_MODE` as the environment variable, which can be set to a specific value like `x`.

By default, when you run `flask run`, the environment variable is not set, and when you run `flask my_command`, you can set it explicitly. However, if you want to avoid setting the environment variable every time, you can use a workaround that leverages the fact that the CLI command is executed in the same process as the Flask app. This means you can pass parameters to the `create_app` function based on the CLI command itself.

One way to do this is by using a custom argument parser within the CLI command. You can define a `@click.option` or `@click.argument` to accept parameters that are passed directly to the CLI command. These parameters can then be used to modify the behavior of the `create_app` function.

Alternatively, you can use the `flask` command with a custom script that allows you to pass arguments to the CLI command. This script can then call the `create_app` function with different parameters based on the arguments provided.

Another approach is to use a context object or a configuration class that can be modified based on the CLI command. This allows you to pass different configuration values to the `create_app` function, depending on whether the app is being run as a server or as a CLI command.

You can also use the `flask` command with a custom entry point that allows you to pass arguments to the CLI command. This can be done by modifying the `FLASK_APP` environment variable to point to a script that handles the CLI command differently based on the arguments passed.
